data.output:
180	| Real-doc usefulness | A manual review of the full-doc scan marks at least 70% of findings useful. |
181	| Finding volume | Full `docs/**` scan is sparse enough for review: target under 1 finding per 1,000 words until rules prove higher precision. |
182	
183	How we know it works:
184	
185	- Golden tests lock expected normalized findings for the corpus.
186	- A full `docs/**` report is reviewed and labeled before enabling workflow
187	  hooks.
188	- Each rule has both positive and negative examples; no rule ships with only
189	  synthetic positives.
190	- Token-count improvements are reported separately from clarity findings so
191	  terse-but-ambiguous rewrites do not pass as quality improvements.
192	
193	### Phase 5: Agent Skill Upgrade
194	
195	Make agents use the prose workflow without reminders.
196	
197	Work:
198	
199	- Update `human-writing-support` to treat Vale-backed `ddx doc prose` as the
200	  normal post-edit check.
201	- Add examples for:
202	  - unsupported benefit claim -> concrete rewrite
203	  - AI-slop paragraph -> shorter actionable sentence
204	  - false positive in a table/path/API sample -> leave unchanged
205	  - token-cost edit -> remove filler while preserving meaning
206	- Instruct agents to rerun `ddx doc prose --changed` after edits.
207	- Instruct agents to report intentional exceptions only when findings remain.
208	
209	Acceptance:
210	
211	- Skill validation passes.
212	- A docs-edit dry run shows the agent runs the prose check, applies obvious
213	  edits, and leaves legitimate technical prose intact.
214	
215	Skill contents:
216	
217	- Trigger: any writing, rewriting, editing, or reviewing of Markdown under
218	  `docs/`.
219	- Required workflow:
220	  1. preserve the author's intent and DDx terminology;
221	  2. edit the document;
222	  3. run `ddx doc prose --changed`;
223	  4. apply high-signal findings;
224	  5. rerun `ddx doc prose --changed`;
225	  6. summarize remaining intentional exceptions.
226	- Preservation rules:
227	  - keep paths, commands, IDs, frontmatter, headings, table structure, API
228	    names, and acceptance criteria intact;
229	  - do not flatten useful technical density;
230	  - do not rewrite quoted source text unless the task is explicitly about that
231	    quote.
232	- Rewrite guidance:
233	  - replace broad claims with actor/action/artifact/evidence;
234	  - delete filler transitions when the sentence still reads correctly;
235	  - prefer shorter wording when it is equally precise;
236	  - split overstuffed sentences only when doing so improves reviewability;
237	  - leave a finding unresolved when the flagged text is precise and explain why.
238	- Examples:
239	  - unsupported benefit claim before/after;
240	  - AI-slop paragraph before/after;
241	  - token-cost reduction before/after with word-count delta;
242	  - false positive that should be ignored;
243	  - table/path/API sample that must not be rewritten.
244	
245	Skill evals:
246	
247	| Eval | Prompt | Pass condition |
248	|---|---|---|
249	| `docs-edit-runs-check` | Edit a small doc under `docs/`. | Agent runs `ddx doc prose --changed` after editing. |
250	| `fixes-ai-slop` | Given a doc with broad LLM-default claims. | Agent rewrites to concrete actor/action/evidence and reruns the check. |
251	| `preserves-technical-structure` | Given a doc with paths, commands, tables, IDs, and AC. | Agent leaves structure intact and does not chase false positives. |
252	| `reduces-token-cost` | Given filler-heavy prose with clear meaning. | Agent shortens prose without losing constraints or evidence. |
253	| `reports-exceptions` | Given a legitimate finding that should remain. | Agent states the exception and rationale instead of silently ignoring it. |
254	
255	How we know the skill works:
256	
257	- The eval prompts run through the same skill-loading path agents use in normal
258	  work.
259	- Each eval checks behavior, not exact prose: command was run, findings were
260	  handled, structure was preserved, and remaining exceptions were explained.
261	- A failed eval produces a concrete skill patch, not a checker-rule patch,
262	  unless the underlying deterministic finding is wrong.
263	
264	### Phase 6: Workflow Hooks
265	
266	Run prose checks in normal DDx work without making users ask.
267	
268	Work:
269

1	---
2	name: human-writing-support
3	description: Support DDx prose work by preserving voice, improving execution-useful clarity, applying deterministic prose findings with judgment, and keeping DDx terminology, headings, tables, paths, commands, and legitimate lists intact.
4	---
5	
6	# Human Writing Support
7	
8	Use this skill when writing, rewriting, editing, or reviewing DDx prose under
9	`docs/`.
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This is guidance for better human writing, not an AI detector and not a
14	detector bypass tool. The goal is prose that helps a maintainer or agent
15	execute, review, or trust a DDx document.
16	
17	## Core Rules
18	
19	- Preserve the author's voice unless the task asks for a new tone.
20	- Preserve exact terminology, APIs, filenames, labels, commands, paths,
21	  acceptance criteria, headings, tables, and legitimate technical lists.
22	- Prefer specific, checkable detail over generic importance language.
23	- Make small local edits when the prose is mostly right.
24	- Do not treat deterministic prose findings as banned-word rules.
25	
26	## Using `ddx doc prose`
27	
28	Run `ddx doc prose --changed` after editing Markdown under `docs/`.
29	
30	Use a finding when it identifies one of these problems:
31	
32	- A broad claim gives no concrete behavior, boundary, command, artifact,
33	  measurement, or acceptance evidence.
34	- The prose hides the actor, action, consequence, or review target.
35	- The wording replaces a DDx concept with a vague substitute.
36	- Repeated or filler text makes the document harder to execute.
37	- Common AI-slop constructions make the text sound polished while omitting the
38	  actor, action, artifact, boundary, measurement, or evidence.
39	
40	Ignore or correct the checker when the flagged text is already precise, such
41	as a path, command, table cell, API name, quoted contract, empirical claim, or
42	canonical DDx term.
43

1	---
2	name: human-writing-support
3	description: Support DDx prose work by preserving voice, improving execution-useful clarity, applying deterministic prose findings with judgment, and keeping DDx terminology, headings, tables, paths, commands, and legitimate lists intact.
4	---
5	
6	# Human Writing Support
7	
8	Use this skill when you are writing, rewriting, editing, or reviewing DDx prose:
9	specs, ADRs, beads, docs, website copy, release notes, and other public text.
10	
11	## What This Is
12	
13	This is guidance for better human writing, not an AI detector and not a
14	detector bypass tool. The goal is prose that helps a maintainer or agent
15	execute, review, or trust a DDx document.
16	
17	## Core Guidance
18	
19	- Preserve the user's voice unless the text is explicitly asking for a new tone.
20	- Prefer specific, checkable detail over generic importance language.
21	- Keep technical precision intact; do not collapse names, commands, paths,
22	  numbers, or constraints into vague summaries.
23	- Keep legitimate headings, lists, tables, code blocks, and DDx terminology when
24	  they improve clarity.
25	- If a deterministic prose check is available, treat it as advisory evidence,
26	  not as a banned-word oracle. Fix findings that make the doc less executable
27	  or reviewable; leave correct technical wording intact.
28	
29	## Using `ddx doc prose`
30	
31	Run `ddx doc prose --changed` after editing Markdown under `docs/`.
32	
33	Use a finding when it identifies one of these problems:
34	
35	- The sentence makes a broad claim but gives no concrete behavior, boundary,
36	  command, artifact, measurement, or acceptance evidence.
37	- The prose hides the actor, action, consequence, or review target.
38	- The wording replaces a DDx concept with a vague substitute.
39	- Repeated or filler text makes the document harder to execute.
40	
41	Do not "fix" a finding by weakening correct technical prose. Paths, headings,
42	tables, commands, API names, quoted contract language, and precise terms are
43	often supposed to be dense.
44	
45	## By Prose Type
46	
47	### Technical prose
48	
49	- Preserve exact terminology, APIs, filenames, labels, and acceptance criteria.
50	- Prefer edits that sharpen structure and specificity over broad rewrites.
51	- Do not remove a technical list just because it looks dense; dense can be
52	  correct.
53	
54	### Planning prose
55	
56	- Keep decision boundaries, dependencies, risks, and open questions visible.
57	- Preserve explicit non-scope statements and acceptance criteria.
58	- Avoid turning a plan into motivational language or a vague vision statement.
59	
60	### Public prose
61	
62	- Optimize for readability, voice, and concrete claims.
63	- Remove generic filler, but keep factual caveats and required qualifiers.
64	- Make the prose sound like a person who knows the subject, not a template.
65	
66	## Editing Checks
67	
68	- Ask whether each sentence adds a specific fact, decision, constraint, or
69	  reviewable implication.
70	- Replace broad claims with evidence, examples, concrete outcomes, or named
71	  artifacts.
72	- Keep a writer's intent unless the text is internally inconsistent or plainly
73	  wrong.
74	- Prefer small, local edits over full rewrites when the original already has a
75	  strong voice.
76

./scripts/eval-skill.sh
---
cli/Makefile:20:.PHONY: all build clean test test-full test-perf coverage-ratchet lint deps help obsidian-migrate obsidian-validate obsidian-test obsidian-dry-run obsidian-nav obsidian-revert bead-schema skill-schema gendocs frontend-build frontend-rebuild frontend-verify copy-skills eval-skill bench-graphql

cli/Makefile:289:eval-skill: copy-skills
cli/Makefile:290:	@cd .. && scripts/eval-skill.sh

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# eval-skill.sh — validate the ddx skill and optionally drive routing evals
3	# against live harnesses.
4	#
5	# Usage:
6	#   scripts/eval-skill.sh --validate            # structural spec conformance only
7	#   scripts/eval-skill.sh                       # validate + routing eval on claude + codex
8	#   scripts/eval-skill.sh --harnesses=claude,codex
9	#   HARNESSES=claude scripts/eval-skill.sh      # env var also works
10	#
11	# Exit codes:
12	#   0 — all checks passed
13	#   1 — validation or routing check failed
14	#   2 — invocation error (missing files, no ddx binary, etc.)
15	#
16	# Validation (always runs):
17	#   - SKILL.md exists at skills/ddx/SKILL.md
18	#   - Frontmatter contains only `name` + `description` (portable agentskills.io minimum)
19	#   - Body is under 500 lines
20	#   - All reference/*.md files linked from SKILL.md exist
21	#
22	# Routing eval (skipped in --validate mode):
23	#   - For each row in skills/ddx/evals/routing.jsonl, invoke
24	#     `ddx agent run --harness <h> --text <phrase>` and check that the
25	#     response mentions the expected reference file OR the expected CLI command.
26	
27	set -euo pipefail
28	
29	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
30	SKILL_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/skills/ddx"
31	SKILL_MD="$SKILL_DIR/SKILL.md"
32	EVAL_FILE="$SKILL_DIR/evals/routing.jsonl"
33	
34	VALIDATE_ONLY=0
35	HARNESSES="${HARNESSES:-claude,codex}"
36	
37	for arg in "$@"; do
38	  case "$arg" in
39	    --validate) VALIDATE_ONLY=1 ;;
40	    --harnesses=*) HARNESSES="${arg#*=}" ;;
41	    -h|--help)
42	      sed -n '1,30p' "$0"
43	      exit 0
44	      ;;
45	  esac
46	done
47	
48	fail() {
49	  echo "FAIL: $*" >&2
50	  exit 1
51	}
52	
53	info() {
54	  echo "$*"
55	}
56	
57	# --- Validation (always) ---
58	
59	info "==> Validating $SKILL_MD against agentskills.io spec"
60	
61	[[ -f "$SKILL_MD" ]] || fail "SKILL.md not found at $SKILL_MD"
62	
63	# Extract frontmatter (between first two --- lines)
64	frontmatter="$(awk '/^---$/{c++; if (c==1) next; if (c==2) exit} c==1' "$SKILL_MD")"
65	[[ -n "$frontmatter" ]] || fail "SKILL.md has no frontmatter"
66	
67	# Check required fields
68	echo "$frontmatter" | grep -qE '^name:[[:space:]]*ddx[[:space:]]*$' \
69	  || fail "SKILL.md frontmatter missing or wrong 'name: ddx'"
70	echo "$frontmatter" | grep -qE '^description:[[:space:]]*' \
71	  || fail "SKILL.md frontmatter missing 'description:'"
72	
73	# Check for Claude-Code-only fields that would break portability
74	for forbidden in argument-hint when_to_use disable-model-invocation user-invocable allowed-tools context: paths: hooks: model: effort: agent:; do
75	  if echo "$frontmatter" | grep -qE "^${forbidden}[[:space:]]*"; then
76	    fail "SKILL.md frontmatter contains non-portable field '$forbidden' (agentskills.io minimum is name + description only)"
77	  fi
78	done
79	
80	# Body under 500 lines (per Anthropic progressive-disclosure guidance)
81	body_lines="$(awk '/^---$/{c++; if (c==2) { found=1; next }} found' "$SKILL_MD" | wc -l | tr -d '[:space:]')"
82	if [[ "$body_lines" -gt 500 ]]; then
83	  fail "SKILL.md body is $body_lines lines (>500). Move detail to reference/*.md files."
84	fi
85	
86	# All reference/*.md files linked from SKILL.md must exist
87	missing_refs=""
88	while IFS= read -r ref; do
89	  if [[ ! -f "$SKILL_DIR/$ref" ]]; then
90	    missing_refs+="$ref "
91	  fi
92	done < <(grep -oE 'reference/[a-z0-9_-]+\.md' "$SKILL_MD" | sort -u)
93	[[ -z "$missing_refs" ]] || fail "SKILL.md links to missing reference files: $missing_refs"
94	
95	info "✓ Validation passed ($body_lines body lines, portable frontmatter, refs exist)"
96	
97	if [[ "$VALIDATE_ONLY" == "1" ]]; then
98	  exit 0
99	fi
100	
101	# --- Routing eval ---
102	
103	[[ -f "$EVAL_FILE" ]] || fail "Eval fixtures missing at $EVAL_FILE"
104	command -v ddx >/dev/null 2>&1 || fail "ddx binary not on PATH; routing eval needs it"
105	command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1 || fail "jq required for reading routing.jsonl"
106	
107	# --- Fixture schema validation ---
108	
109	info "==> Validating routing fixture schema ($EVAL_FILE)"
110	
111	schema_fail=0
112	row_count=0
113	while IFS= read -r line; do
114	  [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue
115	  row_count=$((row_count + 1))
116	  for field in phrase mode references queue_commands tracker_mutation_allowed code_edits_allowed expected_next_action; do
117	    if ! echo "$line" | jq -e "has(\"$field\")" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
118	      echo "  SCHEMA FAIL row $row_count: missing field '$field'" >&2
119	      schema_fail=1
120	    fi
121	  done
122	  # references and queue_commands must be arrays
123	  for arr_field in references queue_commands; do
124	    arr_type="$(echo "$line" | jq -r ".${arr_field} | type" 2>/dev/null)"
125	    if [[ "$arr_type" != "array" ]]; then
126	      echo "  SCHEMA FAIL row $row_count: '$arr_field' must be an array (got $arr_type)" >&2
127	      schema_fail=1
128	    fi
129	  done
130	  # tracker_mutation_allowed and code_edits_allowed must be booleans
131	  for bool_field in tracker_mutation_allowed code_edits_allowed; do
132	    bool_type="$(echo "$line" | jq -r ".${bool_field} | type" 2>/dev/null)"
133	    if [[ "$bool_type" != "boolean" ]]; then
134	      echo "  SCHEMA FAIL row $row_count: '$bool_field' must be a boolean (got $bool_type)" >&2
135	      schema_fail=1
136	    fi
137	  done
138	done < "$EVAL_FILE"
139	
140	[[ "$schema_fail" -eq 0 ]] || fail "routing.jsonl schema validation failed"
141	[[ "$row_count" -ge 15 ]] || fail "routing.jsonl must have at least 15 rows (got $row_count)"
142	info "✓ Fixture schema valid ($row_count rows)"
143	
144	# --- Harness routing eval ---
145	
146	info "==> Running routing evals against harnesses: $HARNESSES"
147	
148	fail_count=0
149	pass_count=0
150	IFS=',' read -ra harness_list <<< "$HARNESSES"
151	
152	while IFS= read -r line; do
153	  [[ -z "$line" ]] && continue
154	  phrase="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.phrase')"
155	  mode="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.mode')"
156	  expected_next_action="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.expected_next_action')"
157	
158	  for h in "${harness_list[@]}"; do
159	    # Skip harnesses that aren't available (avoid spurious failures in CI)
160	    if ! ddx agent list 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^$h"; then
161	      info "  (skip: harness $h not available)"
162	      continue
163	    fi
164	
165	    response="$(ddx agent run --harness "$h" --text "$phrase" 2>&1 || true)"
166	
167	    # Check if response mentions any reference file from the fixture
168	    matched=0
169	    while IFS= read -r ref; do
170	      [[ -z "$ref" ]] && continue
171	      if echo "$response" | grep -qF "$ref"; then
172	        matched=1
173	        break
174	      fi
175	    done < <(echo "$line" | jq -r '.references[]' 2>/dev/null)
176	
177	    # If no ref matched, check queue_commands
178	    if [[ "$matched" -eq 0 ]]; then
179	      while IFS= read -r cmd; do
180	        [[ -z "$cmd" ]] && continue
181	        if echo "$response" | grep -qF "$cmd"; then
182	          matched=1
183	          break
184	        fi
185	      done < <(echo "$line" | jq -r '.queue_commands[]' 2>/dev/null)
186	    fi
187	
188	    if [[ "$matched" -eq 1 ]]; then
189	      pass_count=$((pass_count+1))
190	    else
191	      fail_count=$((fail_count+1))
192	      refs_str="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '[.references[]] | join(", ")' 2>/dev/null)"
193	      cmds_str="$(echo "$line" | jq -r '[.queue_commands[]] | join(", ")' 2>/dev/null)"
194	      echo "  ✗ [$h] '$phrase' (mode=$mode, next=$expected_next_action)" >&2
195	      echo "      expected ref in: ${refs_str:-none}" >&2
196	      echo "      expected cmd in: ${cmds_str:-none}" >&2
197	    fi
198	  done
199	done < "$EVAL_FILE"
200	
201	info "==> Routing eval: $pass_count passed, $fail_count failed"
202	
203	if [[ "$fail_count" -gt 0 ]]; then
204	  exit 1
205	fi
206

input.content:
---
name: human-writing-support
description: Support DDx prose work by preserving voice, improving execution-useful clarity, applying deterministic prose findings with judgment, and keeping DDx terminology, headings, tables, paths, commands, and legitimate lists intact.
---

# Human Writing Support

Trigger: any writing, rewriting, editing, or reviewing of Markdown under
`docs/` (specs, ADRs, plans, beads, release notes, website copy).

This is guidance for better human writing, not an AI detector and not a
detector bypass tool. The goal is prose that helps a maintainer or agent
execute, review, or trust a DDx document.

## Required Workflow

After any docs edit:

1. Preserve the author's intent and DDx terminology.
2. Edit the document.
3. Run `ddx doc prose --changed`.
4. Apply high-signal findings.
5. Rerun `ddx doc prose --changed`.
6. Summarize remaining intentional exceptions in the change description.

`ddx doc prose` is the only public prose checker surface; do not invoke
Vale, Hemingway, or other tools directly. Findings are advisory, not
blocking — apply judgment.

## Preservation Rules

Keep these intact unless the task is explicitly about rewriting them:

- Paths, commands, IDs, frontmatter, headings, table structure, API names,
  function signatures, and acceptance criteria.
- Quoted source text (do not rewrite quotations).
- Useful technical density — dense can be correct.
- Legitimate technical lists, code blocks, and DDx canonical terms.

Do not "fix" a finding by weakening correct technical prose.

## Rewrite Guidance

When applying a finding:

- Replace broad claims with actor / action / artifact / evidence.
- Delete filler transitions when the sentence still reads correctly.
- Prefer shorter wording when it is equally precise.
- Split overstuffed sentences only when doing so improves reviewability.
- Leave a finding unresolved when the flagged text is precise; explain why
  in the change description.

## Examples

### 1. Unsupported benefit claim → concrete rewrite

Before:

> This change significantly improves the developer experience.

After:

> `ddx try` now writes per-attempt evidence under
> `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/`, so reviewers can inspect prompt, response,
> and merge result without rerunning the bead.

### 2. AI-slop paragraph → shorter actionable sentence

Before:

> In today's fast-paced software development landscape, it is critically
> important that we leverage best-in-class tooling to ensure our workflows
> remain robust, scalable, and future-proof across all stakeholders.

After:

> Run `ddx doc prose --changed` after editing docs so reviewers see the
> same findings the agent saw.

### 3. Token-cost edit → remove filler while preserving meaning

Before (38 words):

> It is worth noting that, in order to ensure that the bead is properly
> closed out, the agent should make sure to verify that all of the
> acceptance criteria have been satisfied before committing the change.

After (15 words):

> Verify every acceptance criterion before committing; do not close the
> bead with red tests.

### 4. False positive → leave unchanged

The checker flags "must" in:

> AC 6: `lefthook run pre-commit` must pass.

Leave it. "Must" is the correct contract verb for acceptance criteria.
Record the exception in the change description if `ddx doc prose --changed`
still surfaces it.

### 5. Table / path / API sample → do not rewrite

Do not rewrite cells, paths, or API examples even if the checker flags
them. For example, leave this table column intact:

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `ddx bead ready` | List beads with all deps satisfied. |
| `ddx try <id>` | Run one bead in an isolated worktree. |

And leave path / API samples such as `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/` or
`buildAgentRunner(ctx, cfg)` unchanged.

## Reporting Exceptions

When `ddx doc prose --changed` still has findings after the second run,
list the intentional exceptions and the rationale (for example: "AC verb
'must' is contract language", "table cells are canonical command names").
Silently ignoring a finding is not acceptable; report it.
